name: ContextBench
description: ContextBench is the official benchmark and fine-tuning dataset collection for the ContextOS context lifecycle management framework. It provides 300,000 examples across five subsets: multi-task context management (context_bench, 70K), hybrid retrieval evaluation (retrieval_bench, 30K), context compression fidelity (compression_bench, 30K), embedding model fine-tuning (embedding_finetune, 140K), and LLM instruction fine-tuning (llm_finetune, 30K). All subsets are generated programmatically from fixed random seeds using only Python standard library code, covering six professional domains (software engineering, research, customer service, healthcare, legal, finance) and eight context management task types. Each dataset file is a flat JSONL file in the datasets/ directory following the naming convention {subset}_{split}.jsonl.

subsets.name: context_bench
subsets.description: Multi-task context management benchmark covering eight task types (summarize_session, track_state, detect_contradiction, recall_procedure, synthesize_facts, answer_from_memory, identify_gaps, timeline_reconstruction) across six professional domains (software_engineering, research, customer_service, healthcare, legal, finance). Each example contains 10-50 ContextItems with timestamps and importance scores, a task instruction, a reference answer, and the IDs of relevant items. Generated by generate_context_bench.py with seed 42.

context_items.description: 10-50 ContextItem objects; each has id, content, type, timestamp, importance

relevant_item_ids.description: IDs of context items most relevant to the task (2-8 per example)

content.description: Text content of the context item

type.description: One of: observation, decision, statement, action, question, fact, event, note

subsets.name: retrieval_bench
subsets.description: Retrieval evaluation benchmark covering four domains (technology, science, business, medical) and three difficulty levels (easy: obvious lexical match, medium: paraphrase match, hard: indirect/inferential match). Each example contains a natural-language query, a corpus of 50-100 candidate items, and ground-truth relevant and irrelevant item IDs (1-5 relevant per example). Generated by generate_retrieval_bench.py with seed 7.

id.description: Unique example identifier (MD5 hash prefix, 16 chars)

query.description: Natural-language search or task query constructed from domain templates

relevant_ids.description: Ground-truth relevant item IDs (1-5 per example)

subsets.name: compression_bench
subsets.description: Context compression fidelity benchmark covering four domains (technology, science, business, medical). Each example contains a source passage of 200-1000 words, a reference compression at approximately 40% of the original word count, and pre-computed quality metrics (ROUGE-L, Jaccard semantic similarity, composite information_preserved score). The reference compression is extractive: it retains the first two sentences then samples greedily from the remainder to reach the target length. Generated by generate_compression_bench.py with seed 42.

id.description: Unique example identifier (MD5 hash prefix, 16 chars)

compressed_text.description: Reference compression at ~40% of original word count

rouge_l_score.description: ROUGE-L F1 between original and compressed text

semantic_similarity.description: Jaccard word-set similarity between original and compressed

information_preserved.description: Composite score: 0.6 * rouge_l + 0.4 * jaccard, clamped to [0, 1]

subsets.compression_algorithm: Extractive: keep first 2 sentences + greedy sample from remainder to ~40% word count

subsets.name: embedding_finetune
subsets.description: Contrastive triplet data for fine-tuning dense retrieval embedding models (e.g., BAAI/bge-m3, intfloat/e5-large-v2) on the ContextOS retrieval task distribution. Each example contains an anchor (query or context item), a positive (semantically relevant passage), and a hard negative (same-domain passage with overlapping surface vocabulary but different meaning). Generated by generate_embedding_finetune.py.

query.description: Search query or task query used as the anchor

positive.description: Passage semantically relevant to the query

negatives.description: Hard-negative passages: same domain, overlapping vocabulary, different meaning (typically 3 per example)

subsets.intended_use: Supervised contrastive fine-tuning (MultipleNegativesRankingLoss, InfoNCE, triplet loss)

subsets.name: llm_finetune
subsets.description: Instruction-response pairs for supervised fine-tuning of language models on ContextOS context management tasks. Covers all eight task types and all six domains from context_bench. Formatted as Alpaca/ChatML-style {instruction, input, output} triples. The input field contains serialized context items; the output field contains the reference answer. Generated by generate_llm_finetune.py.

input.description: Serialized context items and task-specific input

output.description: Reference response to the task question

train.description: Training split. Used for model fine-tuning, system calibration, and ablation studies.

val.description: Validation split. Used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping during training.

test.description: Held-out test split. Used for final evaluation and benchmark reporting. Not used during generation or training.

features.generation_dependencies: Python standard library only (json, random, re, hashlib, time, os, math)
features.reproducibility: Fully reproducible from fixed random seeds — no external data downloads required

generation.method: Fully programmatic using Python standard library; no external APIs or downloads
generation.estimated_generation_time: 10-30 minutes on a standard workstation depending on subset sizes

known_limitations: All examples are fully synthetic; no real agent deployment data is included
Domain vocabulary is drawn from fixed template pools (~25 sentences per domain), leading to repetitive surface patterns
English-only; no multilingual or code-mixed content
Token counts in context_bench use a character-based heuristic (chars/4), not precise BPE tokenization
Compression references in compression_bench are extractive (biased toward earlier sentences)
Importance scores are uniformly sampled, not derived from real agent access patterns
Hard negatives in embedding_finetune are domain-sampled, not adversarially mined
No deduplication across train/val/test splits; near-duplicates may exist due to limited vocabulary
